dominantFinding: 4,000+ inland water bodies and Fisheries User Groups already active in 80%+ of major reservoirs give Fisheries the strongest institutional foundation for SOC of any department — yet all four Near-SOC schemes stall at T4 because stocking limits and O&M funds are never codified into scheme design.
dominantFailure: Schemes focused on individual aquaculture (cage culture, PM-MKSSY credit) fail T1 despite sustainability potential because they treat fish farming as individual enterprise rather than commons-managed resource. T4 sustainability lock-in is the critical gap for Near-SOC schemes — all 4 lack mandatory stocking limits, water quality monitoring, and O&M reserve funds.

commonsProfile.keyEcologicalContext: 4,000+ state-owned water bodies constitute the natural commons base; FUG governance tradition reduces the institutional cost of T2 compliance to near-zero, making T4 enforcement the binding constraint

structuralFindings.dominantProblem: The Water Commons Advantage — Inland fisheries are inherently commons-based yet private aquaculture subsidies fail to leverage this
structuralFindings.socException: Reservoir/Tank Fisheries Development and Fish Seed Production Centres achieve full SOC through formalized Fisheries User Group governance and mandatory management plans
crossCuttingPriorities: Fisheries User Group Strengthening: All fisheries schemes must converge with FUG governance; link subsidies (PMMSY, PM-MKSSY) to mandatory FUG membership; formalize FUG legal status and bank accounts

Sustainability Lock-In for Near-SOC Schemes: Implement mandatory water quality monitoring (PMMSY, Integrated Aqua Parks, Export Development); establish stocking density limits; create O&M reserve funds (10-12% of capital)

Aquaculture-Reservoir Integration: Individual aquaculture farms contribute 1-2% output to commons reservoir fishery; create Hybrid Farming model combining individual plot with commons water body participation
Marketing Infrastructure Sustainability: Link market access to sustainable sourcing certification; implement cold chain efficiency standards; support women-led processing cooperatives
contextualFactors: Fisheries Department achieves 70% SOC-adjacent rate — second highest among T1 departments — because inland water bodies are state-owned commons and fishing communities naturally form governance bodies
Chhattisgarh has 4,000+ reservoirs, tanks, and water bodies — an extensive commons infrastructure that naturally enables T1 PASS for most fisheries schemes
Fisheries User Groups (FUGs) already operational across 80%+ of major reservoirs, providing ready institutional foundation for T2 and T4 compliance
Tribal communities (32% population) are heavily engaged in traditional inland fishing — strong cultural commons governance practices complement formal FUG structures

schemes.name: PMMSY (Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana)
schemes.objective: Comprehensive fisheries development scheme (Blue Revolution) with Rs. 20,050 Cr allocation targeting increased fish production, modernized infrastructure, fisher welfare, and sustainable fisheries management.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (inland water bodies state-owned commons); T1.2=1 (Fisheries Act + PMMSY mandates Fisheries User Groups); T1.3~0.65 (65% PMMSY budget to commons infrastructure); T1.4=1
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST+women); T2.3=0.5 (partial FUG governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income indicator mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000-20,000/HH/year; T3.3=2 (multiple pathways); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial mandate); T4.2~0.8 (partial E/R); T4.3=1 (annual monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0.5

T1_1.note: Inland water bodies are state-owned commons; PMMSY primarily funds tank and reservoir infrastructure classified as public natural commons.

T1_2.note: Fisheries Act combined with PMMSY guidelines mandates FUG formation; statutory basis gives score 2.

T1_3.note: Approximately 65% of PMMSY budget flows to commons infrastructure (tanks, reservoirs, hatcheries); individual enterprise component is minority.

T1_4.note: PMMSY includes non-excludable public hatchery and reservoir benefits alongside individual components; mixed but commons-leaning.

T1_5.flag: No explicit commons recognition clause; partial mandate fails hard verification.
T1_5.note: PMMSY treats commons instrumentally without formal commons stewardship recognition in scheme design.

T1_6.note: FUG registration is required for subsidy eligibility in most PMMSY components, providing basic governance linkage.

T2_1.note: 90% of beneficiaries are local fishing community members residing in the command-area villages of targeted water bodies.

T2_2.note: SC/ST and women are explicitly targeted with reservation and priority quotas; 50% combined priority satisfies the ≥0.30 threshold with explicit targeting.

T2_3.note: Partial FUG governance exists on paper but FUGs lack full legal autonomy and operational bank accounts; functional:false reflects governance gap.

T2_4.flag: No mandatory gram sabha deliberation; FUG formation is procedural rather than participatory.
T2_4.note: Commons deliberation is absent; FUG elections occur but broader community ratification is not required.

T2_5.flag: No inter-FUG commons coordination mechanism across connected water bodies.
T2_5.note: Schemes targeting individual reservoirs lack upstream-downstream commons coordination protocols.

T3_1.note: Income improvement is a mandated outcome indicator in PMMSY monitoring framework; scheme design explicitly tracks household income gains.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.8,000–20,000/HH/year observed income delta; 14,000 exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Two distinct income pathways — direct fish sales and infrastructure-enabled processing/marketing — are operational under PMMSY.

T3_4.note: Government hatchery supply guarantees and cooperative procurement channels provide structured offtake for fish produce.

T3_5.flag: No mandatory income-floor guarantee; income gains are scheme-design aspirational, not entitlement-backed.
T3_5.note: Income outcomes depend on market conditions; no minimum-income guarantee exists for PMMSY beneficiaries.

T4_1.note: Original evidence of 0.5 (partial mandate) maps to 0 under hard-gate rule; no binding stocking-limit or water-quality rule is codified in PMMSY guidelines.

T4_2.note: Partial extraction-to-replenishment evidence gives rho~0.80; below 1.25 ceiling but T4_1 failure makes T4 overall fail.

T4_3.note: Annual monitoring is required; score 1 meets the threshold of 1 for non-climate-stressed zones.

T4_4.note: No enforcement mechanism exists; subsidy disbursements are not linked to sustainability compliance outcomes.

T4_5.note: Partial O&M provision exists as FUG stewardship expectation but is not backed by a dedicated reserve fund.

T4_6.flag: MISSING: No community-driven commons sustainability governance clause in PMMSY scheme design.
T4_6.note: Scheme lacks a community self-governance mechanism for resource sustainability; FUG role is advisory rather than regulatory.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No mandatory stocking limit; partial mandate fails hard gate
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven commons sustainability governance
upgradePathway: Mandatory Stocking Limit Rules: Every PMMSY tank with written village-approved stocking limits; fish yield ≤sustainable density (4-6 ton/ha/year per species assessment)
Fishery Lease Sustainability Terms: Amend lease conditions to include mandatory aquatic weed management, fish health monitoring, and quarterly compliance reporting
Water Quality Monitoring Fund: Earmark 5% of PMMSY capital for post-project monitoring; independent water quality testing quarterly
Performance-Linked Subsidy Release: Link Phase 2 subsidy disbursements to Phase 1 sustainability outcomes (FUG registered, stocking limits followed, water quality maintained)

references.title: PMMSY Official Portal — Scheme Guidelines and Operational Framework
Department of Fisheries, GoI — PMMSY Implementation Manual
Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — PMMSY State Action Plan

schemes.name: PM-MKSSY (PM Matsya Kisan Samridhi Sah-Yojana)
schemes.objective: Credit and subsidy support scheme for fish farmers and fishers with Rs. 6,000 Cr allocation providing collateral-free loans, interest subsidies, and insurance.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual fish farming enterprise on private/leased pond); T1.2=0 (no commons access right); T1.3~0.10 (expenditure on individual enterprises); T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.85 (village-level fishers); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST); T2.3=0 (no commons governance body)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income via fishing enterprise); Delta-Y: Rs.10,000-25,000/HH/year; T3.3=1; T3.4=0.5

T1_1.note: PM-MKSSY finances individual fish farming enterprises on private or leased ponds; the resource base is an individual enterprise, not a managed commons.

T1_2.note: Credit scheme does not confer any community access right or commons recognition; loan eligibility is individual enterprise-based.

T1_3.note: Only ~10% of expenditure touches commons-adjacent infrastructure; the dominant allocation finances individual pond upgrades and working capital.

T1_4.note: Benefits are fully excludable — loan access is individual; no non-excludable commons dimension is built into scheme design.

T1_5.flag: MISSING: No commons recognition; individual aquaculture enterprise model throughout.
T1_5.note: Scheme architecture is built around individual entrepreneur model with no commons access or stewardship element.

T1_6.note: No FUG linkage or community governance requirement is embedded in PM-MKSSY loan eligibility criteria.

T2_1.note: 85% of beneficiaries are village-level fishers and fish farmers residing in local communities near targeted water bodies.

T2_2.note: SC/ST fishing communities are explicitly targeted with priority access; 50% priority with explicit targeting passes the ≥0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: No commons governance body — FUG or otherwise — is required or formed under PM-MKSSY; all governance is individual borrower relationship with bank.

T2_4.flag: MISSING: No FUG or gram sabha commons deliberation embedded in loan disbursement process.
T2_4.note: Individual credit scheme bypasses community deliberation entirely; no participatory commons governance mechanism.

T2_5.flag: No community water-body management linkage; borrowers operate independently of any collective resource governance.
T2_5.note: Absence of inter-enterprise coordination means cumulative extraction from shared water sources is unmanaged.

T3_1.note: Income improvement through fishing enterprise is the explicit objective; scheme tracks income gains for loan beneficiaries.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.10,000–25,000/HH/year observed income delta; 17,500 exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Single income pathway through individual fish farming enterprise; no commons-enabled diversification.

T3_4.note: Original score 0.5 maps to 1 per rounding rule; partial market linkage exists through local wholesale buyers but is not institutionalized.

T3_5.flag: No structured offtake guarantee; individual borrowers face market price risk with no institutional buffer.
T3_5.note: Income sustainability depends on market prices; no FPO or cooperative procurement provides price floor protection.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule governs individual pond operations under PM-MKSSY; loan covenants contain no water quality or stocking density requirements.

T4_2.note: Extraction-to-replenishment ratio is not monitored under PM-MKSSY; no data collected on cumulative resource pressure from individual borrowers.

T4_3.note: No independent ecosystem monitoring exists; individual enterprise scheme has no commons monitoring framework.

T4_4.note: No enforcement or sanction mechanism linked to ecological compliance; loan default is financial, not ecological, in trigger.

T4_5.note: No O&M reserve fund or stewardship mechanism is embedded in the credit scheme design.

T4_6.flag: MISSING: No community-driven governance mechanism; entire scheme operates on individual enterprise logic.
T4_6.note: Absence of collective governance means individual decisions aggregate into unmanaged cumulative resource pressure.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No commons recognition; individual aquaculture enterprise model
MISSING: T2.4 — No FUG or gram sabha commons deliberation
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance mechanism
upgradePathway: Commons Convergence Requirement: Loan eligibility conditional on Fisheries User Group participation and commons water body management engagement
Sustainability Certification: Aquaculture beneficiaries adopt certified sustainable practices (stocking density, water quality protocols) as loan renewal condition
Aquatic Biodiversity Clause: 10% of pond area maintains native biodiversity as mandatory loan continuation condition
Cluster-Level Offtake: Form farmer producer organizations with institutional procurement; collective bargaining raises Delta-Y and reduces individual market risk

references.title: PM-MKSSY Scheme Guidelines — Department of Fisheries, GoI
National Fisheries Development Board — Credit and Subsidy Schemes
Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Farmer Credit Programme

schemes.name: Integrated Aqua Parks Development
schemes.objective: Multi-enterprise aquaculture zones combining fish farming, shrimp farming, seaweed cultivation, and value addition as technological demonstration centers.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A/B (government land or community water bodies); T1.2=1 (community access right); T1.3~0.70 (parks on commons/public land); T1.4=1
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.95 (local beneficiaries); T2.2~0.50; T2.3=1 (park governance committee)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.15,000-35,000/HH/year; T3.3=3 (fish/shrimp/seaweed/processing); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial); T4.2~0.7; T4.3=0 (no independent monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0.5

T1_1.note: Aqua parks are established on government-owned land or community water bodies; the infrastructure base is a managed public natural commons.

T1_2.note: Community access right is established through park allotment procedures; score 1 (community right) as statutory Fisheries Act backing is indirect.

T1_3.note: 70% of park investment goes to shared commons infrastructure — water supply, processing halls, cold storage — accessible to all plot holders.

T1_4.note: Park infrastructure benefits are non-excludable among registered members; shared hatchery, cold chain, and market access are collective goods.

T1_5.flag: No formal commons-resource recognition; park plots treated as individual leases within a shared facility.
T1_5.note: Plot-level individual enterprise model coexists with commons infrastructure but stewardship norms are not codified.

T1_6.note: Park governance committee membership is required for plot allotment, providing basic governance participation linkage.

T2_1.note: 95% of park beneficiaries are local fishers and aquaculture farmers from command-area communities; strict locality targeting enforced in allotment.

T2_2.note: SC/ST communities receive explicit priority in plot allotment with 50% reservation; meets ≥0.30 threshold with explicit targeting.

T2_3.note: Park governance committee is operational with elected representation; functional:true as committee manages plot allotment, water allocation, and shared infrastructure.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha ratification of park governance decisions; park committee operates without broader community deliberation mandate.
T2_4.note: Governance is confined to registered park members; surrounding community has no formal voice in park resource decisions.

T2_5.flag: No inter-park commons coordination; individual parks operate as isolated clusters without ecosystem-level resource management.
T2_5.note: Connected water bodies feeding multiple parks lack coordinated extraction governance across park boundaries.

T3_1.note: Income generation is mandated as a primary outcome; park design explicitly targets household income improvement through enterprise diversification.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.15,000–35,000/HH/year observed income delta; 25,000 substantially exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Four distinct income pathways operational within park design; highest pathway diversity among all fisheries schemes.

T3_4.note: Export market channels and institutional procurement agreements provide structured multi-channel offtake for park produce.

T3_5.flag: No commons income-floor guarantee; park income depends on individual enterprise performance within shared infrastructure.
T3_5.note: Income outcomes are enterprise-contingent; infrastructure access does not guarantee minimum income for all plot holders.

T4_1.note: Original 0.5 (partial mandate) maps to 0 under hard-gate rule; no binding plot-level stocking limits or water quality rules are codified in park operating conditions.

T4_2.note: Estimated rho~0.70 from partial extraction evidence; below 1.25 ceiling but T4_1 failure makes T4 overall fail regardless.

T4_3.note: No independent ecosystem monitoring exists; self-reporting by park operators is insufficient for score ≥1.

T4_4.note: No enforcement mechanism; plot renewal is not linked to sustainability compliance; no sanction pathway for ecosystem violations.

T4_5.note: Original 0.5 maps to 1; partial O&M provision exists as governance committee expectation but without dedicated financial reserve.

T4_6.flag: STRUCTURAL: No independent monitoring framework; zero monitoring score disqualifies sustainability lock-in.
T4_6.note: Absence of third-party monitoring means sustainability claims cannot be verified; self-reporting creates conflict of interest.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No mandatory plot-level stocking limits; partial mandate fails hard gate
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — No independent ecosystem monitoring; self-reporting insufficient
upgradePathway: Ecosystem Health Monitoring: Quarterly water quality monitoring; publish reports publicly; link plot renewal to compliance (independent lab testing)
Biodiversity Requirement: 15% of park area maintain native aquatic biodiversity; no monoculture dominance in any single zone
Park O&M Reserve Fund: 12% of infrastructure cost for long-term maintenance; held in gram cooperative account
Plot-Level Sustainability Terms: All leases include stocking limits and water quality compliance; lease cancellation triggered by violations with 30-day cure period

references.title: Integrated Aqua Parks Scheme — Department of Fisheries, GoI
National Fisheries Development Board — Aqua Park Development Guidelines
Chhattisgarh Fisheries — Aqua Park Implementation Status

schemes.name: Cage Culture in Stone Quarries
schemes.objective: Innovative aquaculture in water-filled stone quarries using net cages for high-density fish rearing; 162 cages operational.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C/A (water is public but cage is individual enterprise); T1.2=0 (no community governance of quarry); T1.3~0.20; T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (local fishers); T2.2~0.40; T2.3=0.5 (partial Fisheries User Group)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.20,000-40,000/year per cage; T3.3=1; T3.4=1
evidence.t4: T4.1=0; T4.2~0.5 (partial extraction awareness); T4.3=0; T4.4=0; T4.5=0

T1_1.note: Although quarry water bodies are public commons, individual cage units constitute private enterprise operations; scheme commonsType is C because the productive unit (cage) is individual enterprise on commons water.

T1_2.note: No community access right or commons governance structure governs quarry water use; cage allocation is individual license-based.

T1_3.note: Only ~20% of scheme expenditure touches shared infrastructure; dominant allocation finances individual cage hardware and fingerling costs.

T1_4.note: Cage benefits are fully excludable — individual operators capture all cage revenue; no non-excludable commons dimension.

T1_5.flag: MISSING: No commons recognition; individual cage enterprise model despite public water body.
T1_5.note: Public water body is instrumentalized for private gain without formal commons stewardship obligation on cage operators.

T1_6.note: No FUG linkage required for cage allocation; individual licensing bypasses collective governance.

T2_1.note: 90% of cage operators are local fishers from communities adjacent to the quarry water bodies; strong locality targeting.

T2_2.note: SC/ST communities are explicitly prioritized in cage allocation with 40% reservation; exceeds ≥0.30 threshold with explicit targeting.

T2_3.note: Partial FUG exists on paper for some quarry clusters; functional:false as FUG lacks legal authority over cage allocation and water quality enforcement.

T2_4.flag: MISSING: No quarry commons deliberation; cage allocation decisions are administrative, not participatory.
T2_4.note: Individual cage licensing bypasses collective decision-making; quarry community has no formal governance voice.

T2_5.flag: No inter-cage commons coordination; cumulative water quality impact of 162 cages is unmonitored collectively.
T2_5.note: Individual cage operators have no mechanism to coordinate on stocking density or water quality across shared quarry water.

T3_1.note: Income generation is the primary scheme objective; cage fish sales income is tracked as the core outcome indicator.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.20,000–40,000/cage/year observed income delta; 30,000 substantially exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Single income pathway through cage fish sales; no diversification into commons co-management revenue.

T3_4.note: Wholesale market buyers and cold chain infrastructure provide functional offtake for cage fish produce.

T3_5.flag: No institutional offtake guarantee; income depends on individual market negotiation without collective bargaining power.
T3_5.note: Individual cage operators face price volatility with no FPO or cooperative procurement providing income stability.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule governs cage stocking density or water quality; cage licenses lack mandatory environmental compliance conditions.

T4_2.note: Partial extraction awareness exists among operators but rho is not formally monitored; mapped to null per 'not monitored' rule, resulting in fail.

T4_3.note: No independent water quality or ecosystem monitoring for quarry water bodies; zero monitoring score fails ≥1 threshold.

T4_4.note: No enforcement mechanism; cage license renewal is not linked to ecological compliance or water quality outcomes.

T4_5.note: No O&M reserve fund or stewardship mechanism; quarry water body maintenance is unbudgeted.

T4_6.flag: MISSING: No commons governance mechanism for quarry water body management.
T4_6.note: Absence of collective governance means 162 cage operators act as independent actors on shared public water without coordination.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No commons recognition despite public water body
MISSING: T4.6 — No community governance mechanism for quarry water body
upgradePathway: Quarry Water Body Governance: Establish Quarry Fisheries User Committee; all cage operators must be registered members
Water Quality Standards: Monthly testing; mandatory quality compliance as cage operating condition; quarterly public reporting
Extraction Limits: Set maximum stocking density based on water volume/renewal rate; extraction/replenishment ratio ≤1.2
Community Benefit Sharing: 10% of cage operator profit to quarry community fund for water quality monitoring and maintenance

references.title: Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Cage Culture Programme
NFDB — Cage Culture Technology Guidelines
PMMSY Component — Cage Culture in Reservoirs and Quarries

schemes.name: Freshwater Aquaculture Development
schemes.objective: Support small/marginal farmers in pond and tank-based fish farming through subsidies, training, fingerling supply, and technical support.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A/B (farmer ponds fed by shared groundwater/village tanks); T1.2=1 (community water use right); T1.3~0.55 (majority on commons water infrastructure); T1.4=1
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.95 (village-level farmers); T2.2~0.60 (SC/ST dominant); T2.3=1 (Water User Group governance mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000-18,000/HH/year; T3.3=1 (fish sales); T3.4=1 (government hatchery supply offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (pond water budget mandatory); T4.2~0.9 (E/R ≤1.0 maintained); T4.3=1 (annual monitoring); T4.4=1 (fund withholding); T4.5=1 (O&M fund 8% capital)

T1_1.note: Farmer ponds are fed by shared groundwater or village tank commons; the productive base is individual pond management connected to the natural commons water system.

T1_2.note: Community water use right is established through Water User Group registration; score 1 (community right) as formal statutory backing is at scheme rather than Fisheries Act level.

T1_3.note: Majority (55%) of scheme expenditure flows to commons-linked water infrastructure, fingerling supply from government hatcheries, and shared training; exceeds ≥0.50 threshold.

T1_4.note: Government hatchery supply and Water User Group benefits have non-excludable characteristics among registered members; partial public good dimension.

T1_5.note: Commons recognition is embedded through mandatory Water User Group formation and water-use right registration; stewardship obligation is formally acknowledged.

T1_6.note: Water User Group registration is mandatory for scheme eligibility; collective governance participation is a hard prerequisite for subsidy access.

T2_1.note: 95% of beneficiaries are small and marginal farmers in village communities directly dependent on targeted water commons; strict locality targeting enforced.

T2_2.note: SC/ST farming communities are the dominant beneficiary group with explicit priority; 60% combined priority substantially exceeds ≥0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: Water User Group governance is mandated and operational; functional:true as WUG manages water allocation, maintenance schedules, and conflict resolution.

T2_4.note: WUG formation requires gram sabha endorsement in most blocks; community deliberation is embedded in the governance activation process.

T2_5.note: WUG coordinates water allocation across multiple user ponds; inter-user commons coordination is operationally functional.

T3_1.note: Income improvement for small and marginal farmers is the primary mandated outcome; scheme monitoring tracks household income gains annually.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.8,000–18,000/HH/year observed income delta; 13,000 exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Primary income pathway through fish sales; single pathway reflects early-stage scheme design with room for diversification.

T3_4.note: Government hatchery provides fingerling supply guarantee and procurement linkage; structured institutional offtake reduces farmer input cost and market risk.

T3_5.note: Government hatchery supply guarantee provides stable input access; scheme design includes institutional safeguard against supply disruption.

T4_1.rule: Mandatory pond water budget per season
T4_1.note: Mandatory seasonal pond water budget is codified in scheme guidelines; per-pond water allocation and extraction limits are binding compliance conditions.

T4_2.note: Extraction-to-replenishment ratio maintained at ~0.90 per WUG monitoring data; well below 1.25 ceiling, confirming sustainable water use.

T4_3.note: Annual monitoring through WUG reports and department inspection meets score 1; satisfies threshold of 1 for non-climate-stressed zones.

T4_4.note: Fund withholding mechanism exists; Phase 2 subsidy disbursement is conditioned on Phase 1 WUG compliance and water budget adherence.

T4_5.note: O&M fund at 8% of capital is physically segregated in WUG cooperative account; dedicated reserve for water infrastructure maintenance.

T4_6.note: WUG provides community-driven governance for commons sustainability; water budget enforcement and collective monitoring are institutionalized.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

upgradePathway: Upgrade T4.3 to Real-Time Monitoring: Deploy simple water level sensors; mobile app for farmers; quarterly reports to Gram Panchayat
Polyculture Requirement: Mandate 2+ native fish species per pond; increases biodiversity and reduces disease risk
Women's Aquaculture Cooperatives: Support women-only associations for joint marketing and processing; target 50% women FUG leadership
Seed Sovereignty: 20% fingerling supply from local government hatcheries; reduce dependency on external suppliers

references.title: Freshwater Aquaculture Development — PMMSY Component Guidelines
Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Freshwater Aquaculture Programme
NFDB — Freshwater Aquaculture Technology and Support

schemes.name: Fish Seed Production Centres
schemes.objective: Establish and support government fish hatcheries and community seed production centers producing high-quality fingerlings for distribution to fish farmers.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (government hatcheries as public infrastructure); T1.2=1 (universal community access right); T1.3~0.90 (entire budget to commons seed production); T1.4=2 (non-excludable public good)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.98 (all fishers equally eligible); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST priority); T2.3=1 (Hatchery Management Committee)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (seed supply mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000-12,000/HH/year (20-30% yield improvement from quality seed); T3.3=1; T3.4=1
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (hatchery production plan with replenishment); T4.2~1.0 (production matches demand); T4.3=2 (bi-annual production audit); T4.4=1; T4.5=1 (O&M fund 10% capital)

T1_1.note: Government fish hatcheries are public infrastructure commons; seed production capacity is a shared public good available to all registered fishers.

T1_2.note: Universal community access right to fingerling supply is established; all registered fishers are eligible regardless of individual landholding.

T1_3.note: 90% of budget flows directly to commons seed production infrastructure and operations; minimal individual enterprise component.

T1_4.note: Fingerling supply from government hatcheries is non-excludable among all registered fishers; classic public commons infrastructure.

T1_5.note: Commons recognition is explicit; government hatcheries are designated public infrastructure with mandated universal access in scheme design.

T1_6.note: Hatchery Management Committee participation is required for priority fingerling allocation; governance linkage is embedded in access protocol.

T2_1.note: 98% of beneficiaries are registered fishers across all communities; near-universal eligibility with only minimal exclusions for non-registered individuals.

T2_2.note: SC/ST fishing communities receive explicit priority access to fingerling supply with 50% allocation reservation; explicit targeting satisfies threshold.

T2_3.note: Hatchery Management Committee is elected and operational; functional:true as committee manages production planning, allocation, and quality oversight.

T2_4.note: Hatchery production plans are deliberated with registered FUG representatives; demand-side community input shapes supply planning.

T2_5.note: Hatchery committee coordinates seed supply across multiple FUGs; inter-commons coordination is operationally embedded in distribution planning.

T3_1.note: Fingerling supply mandate directly enables income improvement; scheme design links seed access to measurable yield gains for fish farmer beneficiaries.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.5,000–12,000/HH/year observed income delta from 20–30% yield improvement; 8,500 exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Single but high-leverage income pathway; quality seed access generates proportionally large yield improvements across all fish farming schemes.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: Government hatchery to all registered fishers
T3_4.note: Government hatchery-to-farmer supply chain provides institutional offtake guarantee; all registered fishers receive fingerling allocation.

T3_5.note: Government hatchery supply guarantee is a structural income safeguard; quality seed access is institutionally protected against supply disruption.

T4_1.rule: Annual hatchery production plan mandates seed replenishment
T4_1.note: Annual hatchery production plan is a codified requirement; broodstock replenishment and production targets are binding operational rules.

T4_2.note: Production-to-demand ratio maintained at ~1.00; hatchery output matches registered fisher demand, confirming sustainable production equilibrium.

T4_3.note: Bi-annual production audit covers fingerling output versus demand and broodstock health indicators; score 2 substantially exceeds threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: Hatchery budget is conditionally linked to production plan compliance; under-performance triggers review and corrective funding conditions.

T4_5.note: O&M fund at 10% of capital is physically segregated; dedicated reserve for hatchery infrastructure maintenance and broodstock management.

T4_6.note: Hatchery Management Committee provides community-driven governance for seed commons sustainability; production plans are community-ratified.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

upgradePathway: Real-Time Hatchery Tracking: Deploy hatchery production tracking system; monthly seed availability reports to registered fishers
Genetic Diversity Expansion: Expand to 3+ native fish species; maintain genetic diversity protocols to prevent inbreeding
Community Hatcheries: Support tribal areas with community-managed mini-hatcheries under FUG governance
Farmer-Hatchery Partnerships: Direct supply agreements between hatcheries and registered FUGs; price transparency mechanism

references.title: Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Fish Seed Production Centre Programme
NFDB — Fish Hatchery and Seed Production Guidelines
Department of Fisheries GoI — Hatchery Development Under PMMSY

schemes.name: Reservoir/Tank Fisheries Development
schemes.objective: Support community-based fisheries management in reservoir/tank network through Fisheries User Group formation, sustainable harvest rules, and community income generation.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (state-owned water commons); T1.2=1 (FUG fishing rights formalized under Fisheries Act); T1.3~0.95 (entire budget to commons reservoir management); T1.4=2 (non-excludable commons benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.98 (fishing communities in command area); T2.2~0.60 (SC/ST + landless); T2.3=1 (Fisheries User Group elected committee)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (sustainable catch income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.10,000-25,000/HH/year; T3.3=1; T3.4=1 (government procurement guarantee)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (sustainable yield limits in FUG management plan); T4.2~0.95 (E/R ≤1.0 maintained); T4.3=2 (quarterly FUG + annual government); T4.4=1 (fishing rights revoked for violations); T4.5=1 (FUG account for O&M)

T1_1.note: State-owned reservoir and tank water bodies are the primary commons; FUG management formalizes the commons governance relationship.

T1_2.note: FUG fishing rights are formally constituted under the Chhattisgarh Fisheries Act; statutory backing gives score 2 (statutory right).

T1_3.note: 95% of budget flows to commons reservoir management, FUG formation, and sustainable fisheries infrastructure; minimal individual component.

T1_4.note: Reservoir fisheries benefits are non-excludable among all FUG members; classic commons public good with managed access rights.

T1_5.note: Commons recognition is the explicit design principle; reservoir water bodies are designated public commons with FUG as the legal stewardship body.

T1_6.note: FUG membership is mandatory for fishing access rights; governance participation is a legal prerequisite for resource access.

T2_1.note: 98% of beneficiaries are fishing community households in reservoir command areas; near-universal locality coverage among traditional fishing communities.

T2_2.note: SC/ST and landless fishing communities are explicitly prioritized with 60% reservation; strong equity targeting with explicit priority mechanism.

T2_3.note: Elected FUG committee is the primary governance body; functional:true as FUG manages fishing schedules, catch limits, and O&M fund with full legal authority.

T2_4.note: FUG annual plans require gram panchayat endorsement; community deliberation is embedded in the annual fisheries management planning cycle.

T2_5.note: Inter-FUG coordination across connected reservoirs is supported through department-facilitated multi-FUG meetings and data sharing.

T3_1.note: Sustainable catch income for FUG members is the primary mandated outcome; scheme monitoring tracks per-household income from reservoir fisheries.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.10,000–25,000/HH/year observed income delta; 17,500 substantially exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Primary income pathway through FUG-managed sustainable fishing; income is collectively governed and equitably distributed.

T3_4.note: Government procurement guarantee channeled through FUG provides institutional offtake for reservoir fish produce; collective selling improves price realization.

T3_5.note: Government procurement guarantee provides a structural income floor; FUG members are protected from complete market price collapse.

T4_1.rule: Sustainable yield limits in FUG management plan
T4_1.note: Sustainable yield limits per reservoir are codified in the mandatory annual FUG management plan; limits are binding and monitored by department.

T4_2.note: Extraction-to-replenishment ratio maintained at ~0.95 per FUG catch records and government stock assessments; sustainably within 1.25 ceiling.

T4_3.note: Quarterly FUG monitoring combined with annual government stock assessment provides score 2; substantially exceeds threshold of 1.

T4_4.note: Fishing rights revocation is the enforcement mechanism; FUG committee can suspend individual member fishing rights for sustainability violations.

T4_5.note: FUG cooperative account holds O&M reserve fund; physically segregated reserve for reservoir maintenance and habitat restoration activities.

T4_6.note: FUG is the community-driven sustainability governance body with legal authority; commons stewardship is institutionalized through elected committee.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

upgradePathway: Real-Time Catch Monitoring: Deploy IoT fish catch sensors; daily catch data via mobile app; real-time sustainability dashboard for FUG committee
Fish Habitat Restoration: Allocate 5% annual FUG revenue to habitat restoration (aquatic weed clearance, shoreline protection)
Women's Fisheries: Mandate 50% women FUG leadership; women-only fishing cooperative for processing and marketing
Cross-Reservoir Monitoring: Create inter-FUG data sharing on fish stock health; early warning system for over-harvesting signals

references.title: Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Reservoir Fisheries Management
Fisheries User Group Framework — Chhattisgarh Fisheries Act
Department of Fisheries GoI — Inland Reservoir Fisheries Development

schemes.name: Fisher Welfare Schemes
schemes.objective: Social security support for fishing communities including housing assistance, health insurance, accident insurance, and livelihood support during fisheries closure seasons.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (housing and insurance are individual welfare benefits); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.05; T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.95 (fishing community targeting); T2.2~0.70 (SC/ST dominant); T2.3=0 (individual beneficiary model)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (welfare scheme, not income mandate); Delta-Y: 0 (income transfer during closure, not livelihood creation); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

T1_1.note: Housing assistance and insurance are individual welfare entitlements delivered to individual fishing households; no commons resource or infrastructure is involved.

T1_2.note: Welfare scheme confers no commons access right; benefits are individual entitlements not linked to commons stewardship.

T1_3.note: Only ~5% of welfare expenditure touches commons-adjacent activities; dominant allocation is direct individual transfers for housing and insurance.

T1_4.note: All benefits are fully excludable individual welfare transfers; no non-excludable commons dimension exists.

T1_5.flag: MISSING: No commons recognition or stewardship linkage; purely individual welfare architecture.
T1_5.note: Welfare scheme design is entirely individual-centric with no connection to commons resource management obligations.

T1_6.note: No governance participation requirement; welfare benefits are individual rights not conditioned on collective governance.

T2_1.note: 95% of beneficiaries are fishing community households; strong community-level targeting based on occupation-based eligibility criteria.

T2_2.note: SC/ST fishing communities are the dominant beneficiary group with 70% priority; strongest equity targeting among all fisheries schemes.

T2_3.note: Individual beneficiary model with no commons governance body; welfare is delivered directly to households without any collective governance requirement.

T2_4.flag: MISSING: No community deliberation; individual welfare entitlement bypasses any collective commons governance process.
T2_4.note: Welfare scheme design treats fishing communities as individual recipients rather than commons governance actors.

T2_5.flag: No commons coordination mechanism; individual welfare recipients have no collective resource management obligation.
T2_5.note: Welfare architecture reinforces individual dependency rather than collective commons stewardship capacity.

T3_1.note: Welfare scheme objective is social security, not income generation; no income improvement mandate or indicator is built into scheme design.

T3_2.note: Zero income delta — closure-season support maintains existing income during regulatory closure but creates no new livelihood income.

T3_3.note: No income pathway exists; welfare transfers are consumption support, not livelihood creation.

T3_4.note: No offtake mechanism — welfare scheme has no productive output requiring market linkage.

T3_5.flag: MISSING: No income generation dimension; scheme is pure social protection without livelihood enhancement.
T3_5.note: Social protection schemes structurally cannot pass T3 without livelihood integration; upgrade requires commons-linked income component.

T4_1.note: No sustainability rule applicable; welfare scheme has no resource extraction or commons use dimension requiring sustainability governance.

T4_2.note: Not applicable — welfare scheme involves no resource extraction; rho is null as commons use is not a scheme component.

T4_3.note: No ecosystem monitoring; welfare scheme has no commons resource use requiring environmental monitoring.

T4_4.note: No enforcement mechanism applicable; welfare benefits are individual entitlements without commons compliance conditions.

T4_5.note: No O&M or stewardship mechanism; welfare scheme design includes no commons maintenance or governance obligation.

T4_6.flag: MISSING: No commons governance mechanism; welfare scheme is entirely outside commons management framework.
T4_6.note: Upgrade pathway requires linking welfare to commons participation to introduce any governance dimension.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.1 — Individual welfare architecture with no commons resource or stewardship dimension
MISSING: T3.1 — No income generation mandate; social protection scheme fails T3 structurally
MISSING: T4.6 — No commons governance mechanism; upgrade requires linkage to FUG participation
upgradePathway: Link to Commons Fisheries: Condition housing and insurance assistance on active FUG membership and sustainable fishing compliance
Seasonal Employment Linkage: Closure-season support linked to commons asset creation and maintenance (habitat restoration, hatchery support)
Commons Management Training: Combine welfare with mandatory FUG governance training during closure season
Asset Sustainability Clause: Housing recipients must participate in coastal commons monitoring; cooperative housing clusters with shared commons infrastructure

references.title: Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana — Fisher Welfare Component
Department of Fisheries GoI — Fisher Welfare and Social Security
Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Welfare Scheme Beneficiary Status

schemes.name: Tilapia Export Development
schemes.objective: Market promotion and export support for tilapia and fish products to international markets; quality certification, cold chain development, export logistics.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (tilapia hatchery commons; breeding stock genetic diversity as shared resource); T1.2=1; T1.3~0.60; T1.4=1
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (tribal/coastal farmer exporters); T2.2~0.50; T2.3=1 (export cooperative governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (export income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.15,000-35,000/HH/year; T3.3=1; T3.4=1 (export market offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial); T4.2: not monitored; T4.3=0 (no independent monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

T1_1.note: Tilapia hatchery commons and genetic breeding stock constitute a shared resource base; breeding stock genetic diversity is a collective commons good managed by the export cooperative.

T1_2.note: Community access right to hatchery breeding stock and export infrastructure is established through cooperative membership; score 1 (community right).

T1_3.note: 60% of scheme expenditure covers shared export infrastructure, hatchery commons, and quality certification systems; exceeds ≥0.50 threshold.

T1_4.note: Hatchery breeding stock and export market access are non-excludable among cooperative members; shared infrastructure benefits all members.

T1_5.flag: No formal genetic commons protection; tilapia breeding stock diversity is de facto commons but not legally protected.
T1_5.note: Genetic diversity of tilapia breeding stock is a commons resource without formal stewardship rules against monoculture dominance.

T1_6.note: Export cooperative membership is required for scheme benefits; collective governance participation is embedded in access.

T2_1.note: 90% of beneficiaries are tribal and smallholder farmer-exporters from local communities engaged in tilapia farming; strong locality targeting.

T2_2.note: Tribal farmer-exporters are explicitly prioritized with 50% cooperative membership reservation; explicit targeting satisfies ≥0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: Export cooperative with elected governance is operational; functional:true as cooperative manages collective quality certification, export logistics, and revenue distribution.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha ratification of export cooperative decisions; governance is confined to cooperative members without broader community deliberation.
T2_4.note: Export cooperative operates as a member-only governance body; surrounding community has no formal voice in resource decisions.

T2_5.flag: No water quality coordination across tilapia farms; individual farm water use impacts shared hatchery water commons without collective governance.
T2_5.note: Tilapia farms share water commons but lack coordinated extraction governance across farm boundaries.

T3_1.note: Export income improvement is the primary mandated outcome; scheme monitors export revenue and per-household income gains for cooperative members.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.15,000–35,000/HH/year observed income delta; 25,000 substantially exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Single income pathway through export market tilapia sales; cooperative infrastructure enables premium international price realization.

T3_4.note: Export market guarantee through cooperative procurement provides structured institutional offtake at international market prices.

T3_5.flag: No domestic market fallback; income depends entirely on export market access which is vulnerable to international certification and trade disruptions.
T3_5.note: Export market concentration creates income fragility; scheme lacks a domestic price-floor mechanism as backup offtake.

T4_1.note: Original 0.5 (partial mandate) maps to 0 under hard-gate rule; no binding water quality or stocking density sustainability rule is codified in export scheme conditions.

T4_2.note: Water quality and extraction ratios are not monitored; rho is null per 'not monitored' rule, resulting in fail regardless of T4_1 status.

T4_3.note: No independent ecosystem monitoring for tilapia farms; zero monitoring score fails ≥1 threshold for non-climate-stressed zones.

T4_4.note: No enforcement mechanism; export license is not linked to ecological compliance or water quality outcomes.

T4_5.note: No O&M reserve fund or stewardship mechanism; tilapia farm commons water bodies lack maintenance financing.

T4_6.flag: STRUCTURAL: No binding sustainability rule and no independent monitoring; both T4.1 and T4.3 fail.
T4_6.note: Export scheme lacks the two foundational sustainability governance elements required for Near-SOC to SOC graduation.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No binding water quality or stocking density sustainability rule
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — No independent monitoring; zero monitoring score
upgradePathway: Sustainability Certification Requirement: Export farms must achieve ASC (Aquaculture Stewardship Council) or equivalent certification as mandatory export license condition
Water Quality Monitoring: Mandatory monthly testing for all export-focused farms; results published on department portal
Genetic Diversity Clause: Maintain 20% genetic diversity mix in tilapia breeding stock; prevent single-strain dominance
Market-Sustainability Link: Establish price premium mechanism for 'Sustainably Grown Chhattisgarh' tilapia; collective GI registration for export certification

references.title: MPEDA — Tilapia Export Development and Market Promotion
Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Tilapia Export Programme
NFDB — Tilapia Aquaculture and Export Promotion Guidelines

schemes.name: Fish Marketing Infrastructure
schemes.objective: Develop cold chain, processing units, and market infrastructure for fish products including ice factories, fish landing centers, wholesale markets, and processing facilities.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (fish market infrastructure government/cooperative-owned commons); T1.2=1; T1.3~0.80; T1.4=1
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.95 (fishing community beneficiaries); T2.2~0.50; T2.3=1 (Market Management Committee)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (market access enables income); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000-18,000/HH/year; T3.3=2 (fresh/processed/export); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial maintenance); T4.2: not monitored; T4.3=0; T4.4=0; T4.5=0.5

T1_1.note: Fish market infrastructure is government or cooperative-owned; cold chain, processing halls, and landing centers are public commons infrastructure.

T1_2.note: Community access right to market infrastructure is established through cooperative membership or registered trader status; score 1 (community right).

T1_3.note: 80% of scheme expenditure covers shared commons market infrastructure — cold chain, landing centers, processing facilities — accessible to all registered fishers.

T1_4.note: Market infrastructure access is non-excludable among all registered trading members; shared cold chain and landing center benefits are collective goods.

T1_5.flag: No sustainability-linked market access conditions; infrastructure commons are available without stewardship obligation.
T1_5.note: Commons infrastructure access is not conditioned on sustainable sourcing; no stewardship recognition in market access design.

T1_6.note: Market Management Committee participation is required for stall allocation; governance linkage is embedded in market access protocol.

T2_1.note: 95% of market infrastructure beneficiaries are fishing community households from local communities; strong locality targeting through priority trader registration.

T2_2.note: SC/ST fishing communities receive explicit priority market access with 50% stall reservation; meets ≥0.30 threshold with explicit targeting.

T2_3.note: Market Management Committee is elected and operational; functional:true as committee manages stall allocation, pricing oversight, and infrastructure maintenance.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha ratification of market decisions; Market Management Committee operates without broader community deliberation mandate.
T2_4.note: Market governance is confined to registered traders; surrounding fishing community has no formal voice in market infrastructure decisions.

T2_5.flag: No commons sourcing coordination; market infrastructure does not require or incentivize coordinated sustainable sourcing from commons fisheries.
T2_5.note: Infrastructure commons and natural fisheries commons are unconnected governance systems; market access and sustainable sourcing are decoupled.

T3_1.note: Market access improvement enabling income generation is the primary mandated outcome; scheme tracks household income gains from improved market connectivity.

T3_2.note: Midpoint of Rs.8,000–18,000/HH/year observed income delta; 13,000 exceeds the ≥3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Three distinct income pathways through market infrastructure; fresh sales, processed value addition, and export channels are all operational.

T3_4.note: Institutional cold chain and export market linkages provide multi-channel structured offtake for fish produce through commons market infrastructure.

T3_5.flag: No income-floor guarantee; market access does not guarantee minimum income for all registered traders.
T3_5.note: Income depends on market volume and prices; infrastructure access improves efficiency but does not protect against demand or price collapse.

T4_1.note: Original 0.5 (partial maintenance) maps to 0 under hard-gate rule; no binding sustainability-linked market access condition or resource use rule is codified.

T4_2.note: Market infrastructure use is not monitored for sustainability metrics; rho is null per 'not monitored' rule, resulting in fail.

T4_3.note: No commons monitoring framework for market infrastructure; zero monitoring score fails ≥1 threshold for non-climate-stressed zones.

T4_4.note: No enforcement mechanism; stall access is not linked to sustainable sourcing compliance or ecological outcomes.

T4_5.note: Original 0.5 maps to 1; partial O&M provision exists as market committee expectation but without dedicated financial reserve fund.

T4_6.flag: STRUCTURAL: No commons monitoring framework for market infrastructure; sustainability link between market access and commons sourcing is absent.
T4_6.note: Market infrastructure commons and natural fisheries commons are disconnected; sustainability governance for market infrastructure is absent.

T4_7.note: Fisheries department is not in a climate-stressed zone; T4_7 supplementary stress protocol is not triggered.

governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No sustainability-linked market access conditions
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — No commons monitoring framework for market infrastructure
upgradePathway: Sustainability-Linked Market Access: Stall allocation conditional on sustainable sourcing compliance; unsustainably sourced products cannot access premium market infrastructure
Cold Chain Sustainability: Equipment efficiency standards; annual energy audits; carbon footprint targets for cold chain operations
Market Infrastructure Health Monitoring: Quarterly facility utilization and equipment condition reports; O&M fund (10% of infrastructure cost)
Women's Value Addition Hubs: Establish dedicated processing facilities for women traders; target 50% of processing infrastructure revenue to women cooperatives

references.title: PMMSY — Fish Marketing and Cold Chain Infrastructure Component
Chhattisgarh Fisheries Department — Market Infrastructure Development
NFDB — Fish Marketing and Value Chain Development
